data.text/markdown:
msde630 Week 1 Agenda

  1. What is data engineering?
    • working definition
    • scenarios
    • core tasks
  2. Pipelines and APIs
  3. project overview and discussion
  4. tools
    • editor of your choice (recommend vscode or PyCharm)
    • uv environment manager
    • strongly recommend Windows users install wsl2 and use Ubuntu 24.04

2. What is Data Engineering?

Working Definition:

Data engineering is the practice of designing and building systems that collect, store, and transform raw data into a usable format that can be used by organizational interests to answer organizational questions.

3a. Pipelines

What is a data pipeline?

At the conceptual level, the concept of a pipeline is quite intuitive. We execute pipelines every day in how we live. Formally speaking,

a pipeline is a sequence of decoupled, yet interdependent, steps that starts with initial inputs and produces or results in a final output.

informally speaking, it's just following a set of steps to accomplish a task.

3b. Core components

A pipeline has 5 core components:
  1. source
  2. destination
  3. stages
  4. inputs
  5. outputs
visually: SOURCE --> output/input --> stage1 --> output/input --> stage2 --> output/input --> stage3 --> output/input --> destination That is the pipeline process. A pipline has additional features, like automation, notifications, healthchecks, monitoring that sits on top of it to ensure it runs smoothly

3d. Building Blocks

  1. Understanding the data source
  2. Collecting Raw data from the source
    • Batch processing
    • Stream processing
  3. Processing and Transformation
  4. Understanding the destination
almost always the destination of your pipeline will serve as the source for a different pipeline

4. APIs

We are going to look at APIs just enough so that you understand what they are, how to look at their documentation, how to access them, because the source data for the pipeline project sits behind an api

4a. What is an API?

  1. API stands for Application Programming Interface
  2. An API defines how systems and processes talk to each other. It defines a set of rules that specifies how they make requests of each other and how they make responses to each other.
  3. Restaurant Example

4b. How does an API work? (Endpoints and Request Types)

API's work on the concepts of an endpoint and a request types. You need to understand what these are and how to work with them in order to programmatically use any api.

4b. How does an API work? (Endpoints)

An endpoint is a URL that contains all of the information that is required for the API to communicate with what is sitting behind it in order to get what you are asking for. There are 4 parts to an endpoint
  1. protocol
  2. domain (or subdomain)
  3. service
  4. parameters (path and query)

4c. How does an API work? (Request types)

Request types are http methods that indicate the type of operation you want to perform. These are similar to database CRUD methods.
  1. GET - used to request data from the endpoint. It is read-only
  2. POST - create a new resource (or record) with the data sent to the endpoint.
  3. PUT - update an existing resource or create it if it does not exist.
  4. PATCH - used to make partial updates to a resource
  5. DELETE - used to delete a resource.
For the pipeline project will be using GET and POST methods.

4d. Demo

URL: https://msde630-demo.class-labs.com/docs scenario: A stakeholder wants a csv file emailed to her every Friday and noon with the following information from all of their books in their book catalog: the title, the author, the book genre, the year published, and the number of pages. The data exists in a postgres database with an api frontend at the URL above Let's take a look at this and understand the requirements, and determine the stages and building blocks and begin building out a rough design diagram.

Define the requirements and scope

The first thing you always want to do is define the requirements and scope. Fortunately, this can usually be taken right from the scenario. Unfortunately, it is typically vague and without specifics, at least to start. The scenario here is pretty decent. She tells us what she wants (a csv file), when she wants it (Fridays by noon), how she wants to receive it (by email), and the content of what she wants (title, author, genre, year_published, #pages) The question is: has she provided you enough details to for you to get her what she is asking for? When I look at the scenario, I see a number of things that are missing.

The building blocks

  1. BB #1 - We know what the source is. It's a database that is accessed through an API.
  2. BB #4 - What is the destination? I can think of two
  3. The things we are not clear on are building blocks 2 and 3. We know a little bit about 2 - we need to get the data from an api, and almost nothing about 3, since we don't even know what the data looks like.
So, let's look at it.
API Walkthrough

Pipeline Documentation Requirements

  • Step #1: Define the requirements
  • Step #2: Identify the data source(s)
  • Step #3: Identify the data destination(s)
  • Step #4: Identify the tools required to build the pipeline
  • Step #5: Describe the transformations required in the pipeline
  • Step #6: Delineate the pipeline stages
  • Step #7: Define pipeline execution cadence

Demo Pipeline Documentation Requirements

  • Step #1: Define the requirements
    • a stakeholder what a csv file emailed to her every friday at noon with the following details about all of the books in their book catalog: title, author, book genre, publishing year, and the number of pages in the book.
  • Step #2: Identify the data source(s)
    • the data exists in a postgres database that can be accessed through an api at: https://msde630-demo.class-labs.com/
    • the folloiwng endpoints contain the data
      • /authors
      • /author/{last_name}/books
  • Step #3: Identify the data destination(s)
    • the only stated destination is an email sent to the stakeholder
    • for the file to be sent, the file has to be at least generated. Initially we will keep it stored.
  • Step #4: Identify the tools required to build the pipeline
    • programming language - Python
    • orchestration - Prefect (required since the pipeline can execute on a defined schedule)
  • Step #5: Describe the transformations required in the pipeline
    • /authors provides author details
    • /author/{last_name}/books provided details about books
    • the data from both endpoints will have to be joined, likely on author.author_id = books.author_id
    • the data will then be formatted as csv and emailed to the stakeholder
  • Step #6: Delineate the pipeline stages
    • get author information and book information from their respective endpoints
    • extract relevant author information (name) and book information (title, genre, pub year, pages)
    • join author and book data (author_id??)
    • format and write data to csv file
    • email file to stakeholder
  • Step #7: Define pipeline execution cadence
    • the pipeline can be run as a batch job Friday morning
5. Project overview and discussion

5.1 api access

  1. api docs: https://msde630.class-labs.com/docs
  2. api base: https://msde630.class-labs.com
    • all endpoints are appended to this
    • example. the /poll endpoint is -> https://msde630.class-labs.com/poll
  3. note: operations can be performed from the docs. Do not use the docs for this purpose, except perhaps to register

5.2 Discussion

The project is scenario based. The pipeline needs to be developed in the context of the scenario. It will expand and possibly change over the course of the next 8 weeks. Refer to the project discussion document in the week 1 resources for details on the project.

6. tools

  1. editor
  2. environment manager

6.1 Editor

There is no required editor for the class. You just need to be able to write python code. Any text editor will do. However, an editor like vscode has some nice python extensions that will make programming the project easier. Another option is PyCharm, a Python IDE that has a lot of helpful features. You can use whatever you want, but I would recommend either of these.

6.2 Environment manager

For your class project you are required to use an environment manager. An environment manager is a tool that creates and manages an isolated space to install specific versions of python and python libraries. We will be using the uv environment manager

Working with uv:

Note use uv --help (or google or ai) to learn how to perform the uv tasks below
  1. go to the following site and follow the install instructions: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/reference/installer/
  2. after installing uv, logout of your shell and launch a new one. uv should now be available for use. To test it, type uv on the commandline a check the output. If it is not automatically added to your path, you will need to Google how to add it to your path.
  3. some of these commands are also in the to-do List in the week one resources
  4. for the project, you will need to do the following:
    • use uv to install python 3.13.11 (if not already installed) and pin that version globally
    • create a project directory. the pipeline project will be developed inside this directory
    • use uv to create a virtual environment inside the project directory
    • use uv to add the following packages: marimo, httpx (or requests), duckdb
